content:
Reversal of decision from 2026-04-17: we picked DuckDB then for analytics, but after benchmarking 40M rows the concurrent analyst queries were the killer — time to move to ClickHouse. Notes from my thinking-out-loud: benchmark on 40,000,000 rows (raw ~60 GB, parquet compressed ~18 GB) ran on an r5.4xlarge-equivalent VM (16 vCPU, 128 GB RAM). Single interactive scans were fine (~1.2 s median), but with 8–12 concurrent analyst queries median rose to 3–4 s and p95 spiked to 18–25 s; we hit OOMs at ~18 concurrent. That pattern kills analyst productivity: 8 active daily, peak 12 concurrent. DuckDB is great for single-user ad-hoc ML, but it’s in-process and doesn't handle our concurrency needs. ClickHouse cluster handled the same workload (3-node test cluster) with p95 ~1.8 s at 12 concurrent and no OOMs. Cost tradeoffs: DuckDB on a single big VM ~€200/month; ClickHouse managed cluster estimate €1,400/month, ops ~3 engineer-days upfront. Business constraints: data is growing ~2M rows/day, retention 1 year, current dataset 40M. Decision: switch to ClickHouse for interactive analytics, keep DuckDB locally for ML/experiments. Migration plan outline, timeline, and rollback below; tell me any gaps.

Planned timeline: start infra provisioning 2026-06-08, data sync and schema tests 2026-06-09 to 2026-06-16, cutover 2026-06-19, 48-hour rollback window.

Migration tasks: provision 3-node ClickHouse (replication factor 2), schema translation from DuckDB SQL, ingest via Parquet->S3->ClickHouse batch load, build materialized views/pre-aggregations for top queries, integrate with Metabase, RBAC and backups, monitoring (p95 query latency, CPU, disk), validate with Alice (analyst), ops: Julien.

Rollout rules: require p95 < 5 s for top queries under 10 concurrent, otherwise revert. Budget cap €1,500/month and up to 5 engineer-days. Any obvious missing steps or risks?
Which exact concurrency SLO do you want to commit to (target concurrent analysts and p95 latency)?

Target SLO: support 10 concurrent analysts with p95 < 5 s for our top BI queries (realistic for day-to-day work). We saw with DuckDB: at 12 concurrent p95 = 18–25 s, OOMs at 18. ClickHouse 3-node proved p95 ~1.8 s at 12 concurrent in my test cluster. Hardware used for tests: 3 nodes roughly equivalent to r5.large each in the PoC; for prod I expect slightly larger nodes (e.g., r5.xlarge) to hit the SLO. Data shape specifics: main table orders_events (40M rows), orders_events daily ingest ~2M; joins to customers (1.2M) and routes (6k). Queries: heavy group-bys and window functions. Validation must include load tests at 10–12 concurrent with Metabase query patterns.
Managed ClickHouse or self-hosted? Managed reduces ops but costs more — which do you prefer?

Prefer managed ClickHouse Cloud (Altinity/ClickHouseCloud) — team is small, ops overhead matters. Plan: managed 3-node cluster, RF=2, autoscale option if available. Estimated cost €1,400/month (fits €1,500 cap). One-off engineering: 3 days infra setup + 2 days for schema/ingest/tests = 5 engineer-days. Migration details I’ll own: 
- Ingest: initial bulk load from Parquet on S3, then near-real-time via 5-min batches (we can push later to Kafka ingestion if needed).
- Schema: DuckDB SQL mostly compatible, but adjust for MergeTree partitioning by event_date and primary key (route_id, event_ts).
- Pre-aggregations: daily/hourly tables for orders_per_route and weekly_volume_by_customer.
- Complex window queries: keep on-demand initially, optimize with arrays/aggregates and consider materialized views after validation.
- Integrations: Metabase (update connection), Python notebooks (keep DuckDB local), RBAC, backups to S3.

Owners: Sam (me) migration lead, Alice (data analyst) validates queries, Julien (Ops) manages ClickHouse account and alerts. Anything else to add?
Which BI queries do you want validated first — list top 5 by frequency/latency so we can prioritize pre-aggregations?

Top 5 to validate (names, rough scan size, current p95):
1) orders_per_route — group by route on orders_events, scans ~30M rows, p95 18 s (highest business priority).
2) latency_histogram — window and bucket over event_ts, scans ~24M, p95 25 s (complex, causes biggest tail).
3) weekly_volume_by_customer — join orders_events to customers, scans join ~4M, p95 6 s.
4) churn_cohort — cohort by signup date, scans ~12M, p95 12 s.
5) ad-hoc filters — variable scans, p95 variable.

Prioritize Q1 and Q2 for performance and Q3 for correctness. Analysts: 8 daily active, peak 12 concurrent. Monitoring: alert p95>5 s, CPU>70%, disk>70%. Proceed with these priorities.
Agree prioritize Q1–Q3 for materialized views/pre-aggregations — do you want near-real-time pre-aggregations (5-minute cadence) or nightly ETL?

Go near-real-time: 5-minute cadence for Q1 and Q3 pre-aggregations in ClickHouse (materialized views feeding aggregated MergeTrees). Q2 (latency_histogram) keep on-demand initially; try ClickHouse window/array functions or create a sampled-preaggregation if needed. Monitoring & alerting: p95 query latency alert at 5 s, auto-escalate to Julien if sustained > 2 minutes; paging rules for CPU>80% or disk>85%. Retention policy: raw MergeTree 1 year, aggregated tables 3 years. Cutover checklist items: 
- Provision managed cluster (2026-06-08)
- Bulk load from Parquet -> validate row counts (2026-06-09)
- Run 10–12 concurrent Metabase load tests against staged cluster (2026-06-10 to 2026-06-14)
- Build MV for orders_per_route and weekly_volume_by_customer (2026-06-11)
- Alice validates queries and sign-off (by 2026-06-16)
- Cutover Metabase connection and announce (2026-06-19)
Rollback plan: keep DuckDB read-only Parquet snapshots on S3 for 48 hours after cutover; if p95 >5 s for top queries under 10 concurrent, revert Metabase connection and restore traffic. Budget: €1,400/month, 5 engineer-days. Owners and dates assigned. Ready to execute unless you see a missing risk.
Two small risks: network egress/ingest costs and Metabase connection limits — do you want me to add a cost line for egress and test Metabase connection concurrency now?
